title: Artificial Intelligence in Social and Political Sciences
text:
Preface
In this course, we explore the history, theory, and philosophy of artificial intelligence. We cover foundational concepts like the Turing machine, Turing’s behavioral test for machine intelligence, and Searle’s Chinese Room argument, which question what it means for machines to “understand” or think.
Building on these ideas, students will learn the core principles of classical machine learning and neural networks, gaining an intuitive understanding of how algorithms learn from data.
The course also examines AI’s role in political science, showing how technologies such as supervised and unsupervised learning, natural language processing, and deep learning are transforming research and society, while raising important questions about power, equity, transparency, safety, and ethics.

title: 1  Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
text:
1.1 The Discipline of Artificial Intelligence
Like the steam engine or electricity in the past, Artificial Intelligence (AI) is now fundamentally transforming our world, society, and industries. How we approach AI will shape the world we live in. AI has the potential to bring many benefits, including better healthcare, safer and cleaner transportation, more efficient manufacturing, and cheaper, more sustainable energy. At the same time, however, it is widely acknowledged that the same elements and techniques that drive AI’s socio-economic advantages can also give rise to critical ethical concerns and introduce new risks or negative consequences for individuals and society (Nikolinakos 2023).
Artificial Intelligence (AI) emerged as a formal scientific discipline in the mid-20th century, building on centuries of philosophical and cognitive inquiry into reasoning, logic, and the nature of the mind. AI became a reality thanks to the development of advanced algorithms, the explosion of digital data, and the rise of high-performance computing infrastructure.
While rooted in computer science, AI is inherently interdisciplinary, with theoretical foundations drawn from philosophy (knowledge and ethics), psychology (learning and behavior), and neuroscience (biological information processing). Modern AI research also critically addresses ethical considerations such as fairness and accountability, as well as broader societal impacts on human rights, labor, and governance. This highlights the vital need to integrate social science perspectives into the design, deployment, and evaluation of intelligent technologies.
Because of its interdisciplinary and multifaceted nature, combined with the diversity of its applications, AI is not easy to define precisely.

title: 1  Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
text:
Definitions (Manyika 2022)

(Human‑centric description) “Artificial intelligence is the ability of a digital computer or computer‑controlled robot to perform tasks commonly associated with intelligent beings (usually humans), including visual perception, speech recognition, the capacity to reason, solve problems, discover meaning, generalize, and learn from experience.”
(Free of human-centricity) AI refers to any system that can perceive its environment and take actions that maximize its chance of achieving specified goals.

title: 1  Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
text:
1.2 Major Subfields of AI
AI is composed of several specialized subfields, each addressing different aspects of intelligent behavior and employing distinct methodologies and tools that have emerged over more than 70 years.


Symbolic AI: Early AI research focused on symbolic reasoning, which represents knowledge using explicit symbols and formal rules, enabling the application of logical operations to these symbols.
Machine Learning (ML): It is a core subfield of AI that focuses on developing algorithms and statistical models enabling computers to learn from data (training stage) and improve their performance autonomously, without explicit programming.
Deep Learning (DL): Deep Learning is a subfield of ML that utilizes large artificial neural networks with multiple hidden layers to process complex data and recognize patterns (unlike neural networks used in classic machine learning models, which usually have only one or two hidden layers).

title: 1  Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
text:
1.3 Data Splitting in Machine Learning
In a standard machine learning pipeline (especially for supervised learning), data is typically partitioned into three distinct subsets to ensure the model is both accurate and able to generalize well to new data without overfitting1.
1 Overfitting occurs when a model captures the noise and random fluctuations in the training data rather than the underlying patterns. While this results in high accuracy on the training set, the model fails to generalize, leading to poor performance on unseen validation or test data.Training Set: The primary portion of the data used to teach the model by letting it learn underlying patterns and adjust its internal parameters.
Validation Set: A separate subset used during the development phase to fine-tune hyperparameters2 (e.g., k in K-NN algorithm, learning rates or architecture depth in deep neural networks) and guide model selection. It acts like a “practice exam,” helping you choose the best model configuration without biasing the final evaluation.
2 In many machine learning models and algorithms there are a number of parameters and settings that we need to select prior to training, independent from our data, that determine how the algorithm proceeds. These are known as hyperparameters.Test Set: A strictly independent “hold-out” set used only once the model is finalized. It provides an unbiased evaluation of how the model will perform on unseen, real-world data.











Tip Comment



To ensure evaluation results remain valid, the model must never be exposed to the test set during training or tuning phases. Using test data to inform design or hyperparameter decisions allows the model to indirectly learn from it, inflating reported performance beyond what it would achieve on genuinely unseen data. This phenomenon, known as data leakage, produces overly optimistic metrics that fail to generalize to the real world.

title: 1  Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
text:
1.4 Learning methods

1.4.1 Supervised Learning
In supervised learning, the model is trained using labeled data, where each input sample is associated with a corresponding target label. The objective is the prediction.

Example: Is it a square?


The Training Data: The process begins with a dataset consisting of various geometric shapes, each associated with a label (pentagon, triangle, circle, square). In machine learning, these labels represent the ground truth and provide the reference outcomes the model aims to learn.
The Learning Process (The “Gears”): The labeled data is processed by an algorithm, which learns underlying associations between the input shapes and their corresponding labels.
Testing on New Data (The “Robot”): The trained model (the robot) is presented with new geometric shapes and predicts what each shape is.


1.4.2 Unsupervised Learning
In unsupervised learning, the model is trained using unlabeled data. The objective is to cluster the data.

Example: Into which cluster does the new geometric object belong?


Unlabeled Training Data: The input comprises a diverse set of geometric objects lacking predefined categories or ground-truth labels.
Learning to Cluster (The “Gears”): The algorithm identifies “hidden” patterns in the shapes, i.e., similarities in structure, size, or edges to cluster them.
Testing on New Data (The “Robot”): When presented with a new shape, the trained model (the “robot”) evaluates it and assigns it to the most appropriate cluster based on the patterns it has learned.


1.4.3 Reinforcement learning
Reinforcement learning involves an agent (robot) interacting with an environment to learn optimal behavior through trial and error. The agent receives feedback in the form of rewards or penalties based on its actions, and the goal is to maximize cumulative rewards over time.

Example: What is the best strategy for arranging the geometric objects into a pyramid?


The model (robot) is given a goal (“choose the shape to build a pyramid”) and must use trial and error to determine which shapes lead to the highest reward.

In late 2013, DeepMind’s Deep Q‑Network (DQN) marked a milestone in AI by using deep reinforcement learning to play Atari 2600 games, with no prior knowledge of the rules or strategies. Applied to the classic Atari game Breakout, where a paddle bounces a ball to destroy bricks, the DQN agent learned effective play from scratch and eventually discovered a “tunneling” strategy—creating a gap in the leftmost part of the brick wall so the ball could bounce behind it and rack up high scores—a sophisticated policy that emerged purely from learning the reward structure, and notably, even the DeepMind researchers had not anticipated this specific tactic until they observed it in the trained agent (Tegmark 2017).

title: 1  Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
text:
1.5 Components of AI Systems
The core of any machine learning system comprises three interrelated components that form a triangular dependency: models/algorithms, data, and computing infrastructure (Reddi 2024).







Models: Mathematical models, algorithms, and methods that learn patterns from data to make predictions or decisions.
Data: Processes and infrastructure for collecting, storing, processing, managing, and serving data for both training and inference.
Infrastructure: Hardware and software infrastructure that enables training, serving, and operation of models at scale.


Each element influences and constrains the others (Reddi 2024).

The model architecture determines both the computational requirements for training and inference and the volume and structure of data needed for effective learning.
The scale and complexity of the data influence the infrastructure needed for storage and processing, while also constraining which model architectures are feasible.
The capabilities of the infrastructure set practical limits on both model size and data processing capacity, establishing the framework within which the other components must operate.

title: 1  Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
text:
1.6 Classification of Artificial Intelligence
Artificial intelligence (AI) can be categorized into three main types based on its scope and level of capability:
1. Artificial Narrow Intelligence (ANI): Also called Weak AI, ANI is the most common form of AI in use today. These systems are designed to perform a specific task or a limited set of tasks with high efficiency. They are not capable of understanding, learning, or applying knowledge beyond their specific programmed scope.
2. Artificial General Intelligence (AGI): Often referred to as Strong AI, AGI represents a level of intelligence comparable to that of a human being. An AGI system would be capable of learning, reasoning, and solving problems across multiple domains, applying knowledge flexibly to perform any intellectual task a human can.
3. Artificial Superintelligence (ASI): ASI denotes a hypothetical form of AI that surpasses human intelligence in all aspects. Beyond mere computational power, ASI would excel in creativity, problem-solving, social understanding, and scientific innovation, potentially transforming every area of human activity.










Type
Alternate Names
Scope/Capabilities
Current Status
Examples/Notes




Artificial Narrow Intelligence (ANI)
Weak AI, Narrow AI
Single task, high proficiency
Widely implemented
Siri, Alexa, Deep Blue, Google Search, movie recommendations


Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)
Strong AI, General AI
Human-level intelligence, multi-domain
Theoretical?
Sophia robot


Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI)
Superintelligence
Surpasses human intelligence, including creativity, social wisdom, and scientific innovation
Hypothetical
Science fiction, ethical debates

title: 2  A short history of AI
crumbs: 2  A short history of AI

title: 2  A short history of AI
text:
400 BCE: The Mechanical Pigeon



In the 4th century BCE, the Greek philosopher and mathematician Archytas of Tarentum reportedly devised a bird-shaped mechanical device known as “The Pigeon,” propelled by a jet of air or steam and said to travel a measurable distance when released from a pivot or wire. The word automaton—from the Greek automaton (“αὐτόματον”), meaning “acting of one’s own will”—was used in antiquity to describe devices that appeared to move without direct human control. From these origins to modern silicon-based systems and drones, a persistent ambition has defined the history of technology: translating biological models and physical laws into engineered systems capable of autonomous or adaptive behavior.


80 BC: The Antikythera Mechanism



In the late 2nd to early 1st century BCE, the Antikythera mechanism — an ancient Greek hand‑powered device often described as the world’s earliest known analog computer — was designed with interlocking bronze gears and dials to calculate and display astronomical information such as the positions of the sun, moon, and planets and to predict celestial events like eclipses.
